reasoning: Based on recent reporting from late July 2025, the Federal Reserve has held interest rates steady at 4.25%-4.50% for its fifth consecutive meeting this year. The latest communications from Fed Chair Powell indicate no decisions have been made regarding the September meeting, but the internal debate is between holding rates steady and cutting them (two board members actually dissented at the July meeting in favor of a rate cut because they felt monetary policy was "too tight"). Reuters confirms that the last rate hike was in July 2023 and the last cut was in December 2024, definitively ruling out any rate hikes in 2025 thus far. Given the current economic context, dissent favoring cuts, and political pressure to lower rates, the probability of a sudden pivot to a rate hike in the remaining 2025 meetings (September, November, or December) represents an extreme tail risk. The Polymarket price of 0.06 in late July corresponds with this highly restrictive posture and the fading, but non-zero, tail-risk probability. With the July meeting now past without a hike, this probability falls even further. I estimate the chance of a rate hike before the December 2025 meeting to be 3%.
